I've ran into this while upgrading some CI to Python 3.13
Apparently a test as simple as `plt.subplots()` will crash specifically on Python 3.13 + Windows
I created a minimal reprod repo, where I run the same test successfully with the following combos:
- Python 3.13 + Ubuntu
- Python 3.12 + Windows

see https://github.com/neutrinoceros/reprod-mpl-win-python-3.13-bug/actions/runs/11257049452

From my non-expert perspective, there are multiple suspects:
- Python 3.13 itself (note that in my reprod I'm using a uv-managed binary from https://github.com/indygreg/python-build-standalone, but I also obtain the same failure with a binary from Github Actions)
- matplotlib
- my own test configuration: I'm not explicitly configuring a matplotlib backend, but never needed to until now, so I'm not sure this would be a solution or a workaround. However, I have verified that doing so *works*. Please feel free to close this issue if this is the recommended solution.

### Code for reproduction

```Python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def test_subplots_simple():
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25693","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25693/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25693/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25693/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25693","id":2101238141,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP859Pll9","number":25693,"title":"TSK: Follow-up things for stringdtype","user":{"login":"mhvk","id":2789820,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3ODk4MjA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2789820?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk","html_url":"https://github.com/mhvk","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359315,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MzE1","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/17%20-%20Task","name":"17 - Task","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":15,"created_at":"2024-01-25T22:01:17Z","updated_at":"2024-09-19T07:14:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

A number of points arose in discussion of #25347 that were deemed better done in follow-up, to not distract from the meat of that (very large) PR. This issue is to remind us of those.
1. [x] The doc for `StringDType` has a size argument that was needed in development but not for actual release. It can be removed. This should be done before the NumPy 2.0 release because it is public API. (Though might it be useful for a short version that only uses arena? see below. Probably can put it back if needed...)
    - https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25856
3. [x] The `add` ufunc needs a promoter so addition with a python string works.
4. [ ] Add a cython interface for the stringdtype C API.
5. [x] It is likely better to use a flag for strings "long strings" (stored outside of the numpy array proper) instead of one for short ones (stored inside), so that an all-zero entry correctly implies a zero-length string (see https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#issuecomment-1910321015)
6. [ ] Refactor the flags in the vstring implementation to use bitfields. This will improve clarity and eliminate complicated  bitflag math.
7. [ ] Possibly, the arena should have more recoverable flags/size information (see https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#issuecomment-1910321015)
8. [ ] Investigate refactoring `new_stringdtype_instance` into `tp_init`
9. [ ] Replace the long `#define` in `casts.c` with templating (or `.c.src`)
10. [x] Replace ufunc wrappers with passing functions into `*auxdata` (see [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#discussion_r1471915449), [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#discussion_r1471928789), [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#discussion_r1471930959), and [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#discussion_r1471932261)) [e.g., `minimum` and `maximum`; the various comparison functions; the templated unary functions; `find`, `rfind` and maybe `count`; `startswith` and `endswith`; `lstrip`, `rstrip` and `strip`, plus `whitespace` versions]. Attempt in gh-25796
11. [ ] Check `array2string` formatter overrides.
12. [x] Adjust error messages referring to "object array" (e.g., `a.view('2u8')` currently errors with `"Cannot change data-type for object array."`).
13. [ ] Have some helper functions that make it easy for `StringDType` ufuncs to use `out` arguments, also for in-place operations.
14. [ ] See whether null-handling code in ufunc loops and casts can be consolidated into a helper function to reduce code duplication.

Things possibly for longer term
- Support in structured arrays (perhaps not super useful, but could be seen as similar to `object`).
- Expose more of the currently private `NpyString` API. This will depend on feedback from users.
- Fix `longdouble` to string, which is listed as broken in a `TODO` item in `casts.c`. isn't `dragon` able to deal with it?
- Add a DType API callback that triggers when the initial filling of a newly created array (e.g. after `PyArray_FromAny` finishes filling a new array). We could use this to trim the arena buffer to the exact size needed by the array. Currently we over-allocate because the buffer grows exponentially with a growth factor of 1.25.
- Might it make sense on 64bit systems, where normally the size is 16 bytes, to have a 8-byte version (short strings up to 7, only arena allocations for long ones; might use the `size` argument...).
- In principle, `.view(StringDType())` could be possible in some cases (e.g., to change the NA behaviour). Would need to share the allocator (and thus have reference counts for that...).
- Dealing with array scalars vs `str` scalars - see also more general discussion about array scalars in gh-24897.

Small warts, possibly not solvable
- `StringDType` is added to `add_dtype_helper` late in the initialization of `multiarraymodule`; can this be easier?
- Can the cases of having and not having gil be factored out, so that one doesn't get the kind of hybrid stuff in `load_non_nullable_string` with its `has_gil` argument.
- to have `dtype.hasobject` be true is logical but not quite the right name.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/colorcet/issues/121","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/colorcet","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/colorcet/issues/121/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/colorcet/issues/121/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/colorcet/issues/121/events","html_url":"https://github.com/holoviz/colorcet/issues/121","id":2187889135,"node_id":"I_kwDOBFp9V86CaInv","number":121,"title":"Slow import on Python 3.12","user":{"login":"bersbersbers","id":12128514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMTI4NTE0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12128514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers","html_url":"https://github.com/bersbersbers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-03-15T07:28:36Z","updated_at":"2024-09-25T10:57:45Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"#### ALL software version info
`colorcet==3.1.0`, Windows 11 23H2, Python 3.12.2 vs. 3.11.8

#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior
`import colorcet` should be fast, but is slow. In numbers:

- Python 3.11 imports in ~0.03s, while Python 3.12 imports in ~0.3s, which is an order of magnitude slower.
- Even worse, with `debugpy` attached, the difference is 0.3s (Python 3.11) vs. 15s (python 3.12), see https://github.com/microsoft/debugpy/issues/1496

I am not 100% sure the two issues are related, but it might make sense to fix the latter and see if it also fixes the former.

It seems one fix for the latter is to reduce the numbers of lines, as proposed in https://github.com/explosion/spaCy/discussions/13266#discussioncomment-8230431 and apparently employed successfully in https://github.com/carpedm20/emoji/releases/tag/v2.10.1 (they now have a 2.4-MB line of code in their https://github.com/carpedm20/emoji/blob/645331c9474aaad7362ccb1d81279724514050fa/emoji/unicode_codes/data_dict.py).

#### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue

`bug.py`
```python
import timeit
print(timeit.timeit("import colorcet", number=1))
```

The main issue still exists: the module imports ten times slower on Python 3.12 than on 3.11.

However, this seems to have resolved in Python 3.13, which imports as fast as 3.11:

```
> python -m pip uninstall colorcet
> python -m pip install colorcet
> python -c "import sys, timeit; print(sys.version); print(timeit.timeit('import colorcet', number=1))"

```

#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior
`import colorcet` should be fast, but is slow. In numbers:

- Python 3.11 imports in ~0.03s, while Python 3.12 imports in ~0.3s, which is an order of magnitude slower.
- Even worse, with `debugpy` attached, the difference is 0.3s (Python 3.11) vs. 15s (python 3.12), see https://github.com/microsoft/debugpy/issues/1496

I am not 100% sure the two issues are related, but it might make sense to fix the latter and see if it also fixes the former.

It seems one fix for the latter is to reduce the numbers of lines, as proposed in https://github.com/explosion/spaCy/discussions/13266#discussioncomment-8230431 and apparently employed successfully in https://github.com/carpedm20/emoji/releases/tag/v2.10.1 (they now have a 2.4-MB line of code in their https://github.com/carpedm20/emoji/blob/645331c9474aaad7362ccb1d81279724514050fa/emoji/unicode_codes/data_dict.py).

#### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue

`bug.py`
```python
import timeit
print(timeit.timeit("import colorcet", number=1))
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25693","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25693/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25693/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25693/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25693","id":2101238141,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP859Pll9","number":25693,"title":"TSK: Follow-up things for stringdtype","user":{"login":"mhvk","id":2789820,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3ODk4MjA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2789820?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk","html_url":"https://github.com/mhvk","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359315,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MzE1","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/17%20-%20Task","name":"17 - Task","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":14,"created_at":"2024-01-25T22:01:17Z","updated_at":"2024-06-18T18:57:39Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

A number of points arose in discussion of #25347 that were deemed better done in follow-up, to not distract from the meat of that (very large) PR. This issue is to remind us of those.
1. [x] The doc for `StringDType` has a size argument that was needed in development but not for actual release. It can be removed. This should be done before the NumPy 2.0 release because it is public API. (Though might it be useful for a short version that only uses arena? see below. Probably can put it back if needed...)
    - https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25856
3. [x] The `add` ufunc needs a promoter so addition with a python string works.
4. [ ] Add a cython interface for the stringdtype C API.
5. [x] It is likely better to use a flag for strings "long strings" (stored outside of the numpy array proper) instead of one for short ones (stored inside), so that an all-zero entry correctly implies a zero-length string (see https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#issuecomment-1910321015)
6. [ ] Refactor the flags in the vstring implementation to use bitfields. This will improve clarity and eliminate complicated  bitflag math.
7. [ ] Possibly, the arena should have more recoverable flags/size information (see https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#issuecomment-1910321015)
8. [ ] Investigate refactoring `new_stringdtype_instance` into `tp_init`
9. [ ] Replace the long `#define` in `casts.c` with templating (or `.c.src`)
10. [x] Replace ufunc wrappers with passing functions into `*auxdata` (see [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#discussion_r1471915449), [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#discussion_r1471928789), [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#discussion_r1471930959), and [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25347#discussion_r1471932261)) [e.g., `minimum` and `maximum`; the various comparison functions; the templated unary functions; `find`, `rfind` and maybe `count`; `startswith` and `endswith`; `lstrip`, `rstrip` and `strip`, plus `whitespace` versions]. Attempt in gh-25796
11. [ ] Check `array2string` formatter overrides.
12. [x] Adjust error messages referring to "object array" (e.g., `a.view('2u8')` currently errors with `"Cannot change data-type for object array."`).
13. [ ] Have some helper functions that make it easy for `StringDType` ufuncs to use `out` arguments, also for in-place operations.
14. [ ] See whether null-handling code in ufunc loops and casts can be consolidated into a helper function to reduce code duplication.

Things possibly for longer term
- Support in structured arrays (perhaps not super useful, but could be seen as similar to `object`).
- Expose more of the currently private `NpyString` API. This will depend on feedback from users.
- Fix `longdouble` to string, which is listed as broken in a `TODO` item in `casts.c`. isn't `dragon` able to deal with it?
- Add a DType API callback that triggers when the initial filling of a newly created array (e.g. after `PyArray_FromAny` finishes filling a new array). We could use this to trim the arena buffer to the exact size needed by the array. Currently we over-allocate because the buffer grows exponentially with a growth factor of 1.25.
- Might it make sense on 64bit systems, where normally the size is 16 bytes, to have a 8-byte version (short strings up to 7, only arena allocations for long ones; might use the `size` argument...).
- In principle, `.view(StringDType())` could be possible in some cases (e.g., to change the NA behaviour). Would need to share the allocator (and thus have reference counts for that...).
- Dealing with array scalars vs `str` scalars - see also more general discussion about array scalars in gh-24897.

Small warts, possibly not solvable
- `StringDType` is added to `add_dtype_helper` late in the initialization of `multiarraymodule`; can this be easier?
- Can the cases of having and not having gil be factored out, so that one doesn't get the kind of hybrid stuff in `load_non_nullable_string` with its `has_gil` argument.
- to have `dtype.hasobject` be true is logical but not quite the right name.

`Series[Timestamp].diff()` returns `Series[Timedelta]` but stubs indicate it's `Series[Timestamp]` instead.

**To Reproduce**
```python
# python bug.py & mypy bug.py & pyright bug.py
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, reveal_type

import pandas as pd

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from pandas.core.series import TimedeltaSeries, TimestampSeries

def fun1(times: pd.Series[pd.Timestamp]) -> pd.Series[pd.Timedelta]:
    reveal_type(times)
    reveal_type(times.diff())
    print(type(times[1]))
    print(type(times.diff()[1]))
    return times.diff()

def fun2(times: TimestampSeries) -> TimedeltaSeries:
    reveal_type(times)
    reveal_type(times.diff())
    print(type(times[1]))
    print(type(times.diff()[1]))
    return times.diff()

times = pd.Series([pd.Timestamp(0), pd.Timestamp(0)])
fun1(times)
fun2(times)
```

Output:

I minimized the code into this example:

```python
# python bug.py & mypy bug.py & pyright bug.py
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import reveal_type

import pandas as pd

def fun(stamps: pd.Series[pd.Timestamp]) -> pd.Series[pd.Timedelta]:
    reveal_type(stamps)
    print(type(stamps[1]))

    deltas = stamps.diff()

    reveal_type(deltas)
    print(type(deltas[1]))

    return deltas

times = pd.Series([pd.Timestamp(0), pd.Timestamp(0)])
fun(times)
```

`python` says:
```
Runtime type is 'Series'
<class 'pandas._libs.tslibs.timestamps.Timestamp'>
Runtime type is 'Series'
<class 'pandas._libs.tslibs.timedeltas.Timedelta'>
```

So runtime behavior is as expected.

However, `mypy` (as `pyright`) thinks `deltas` is a series of `float` still:

```
bug.py:9: note: Revealed type is "pandas.core.series.Series[pandas._libs.tslibs.timestamps.Timestamp]"

(Cross-post from  https://stackoverflow.com/q/77451924/)

I am getting overflow `RuntimeWarning`s for an array of 9 elements but not for an array of 8 (or 200, ...) elements. It seems I get warnings iff I have more than 8 zeros and their number is not divisible by 4.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

# This works fine
np.zeros(1) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(4) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(7) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(8) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(12) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(16) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(100) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(200) / np.float64(1e-309)

# This gives "RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in divide"
np.zeros(9) / np.float64(1e-309)
```


### Error message:

```shell
<stdin>:1: RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in divide
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/colorcet/issues/121","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/colorcet","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/colorcet/issues/121/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/colorcet/issues/121/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/colorcet/issues/121/events","html_url":"https://github.com/holoviz/colorcet/issues/121","id":2187889135,"node_id":"I_kwDOBFp9V86CaInv","number":121,"title":"Slow import on Python 3.12","user":{"login":"bersbersbers","id":12128514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMTI4NTE0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12128514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers","html_url":"https://github.com/bersbersbers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-03-15T07:28:36Z","updated_at":"2024-03-15T07:28:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"#### ALL software version info
`colorcet==3.1.0`, Windows 11 23H2, Python 3.12.2 vs. 3.11.8

#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior
`import colorcet` should be fast, but is slow. In numbers:

- Python 3.11 imports in ~0.03s, while Python 3.12 imports in ~0.3s, which is an order of magnitude slower.
- Even worse, with `debugpy` attached, the difference is 0.3s (Python 3.11) vs. 15s (python 3.12), see https://github.com/microsoft/debugpy/issues/1496

I am not 100% sure the two issues are related, but it might make sense to fix the latter and see if it also fixes the former.

It seems one fix for the latter is to reduce the numbers of lines, as proposed in https://github.com/explosion/spaCy/discussions/13266#discussioncomment-8230431 and apparently employed successfully in https://github.com/carpedm20/emoji/releases/tag/v2.10.1 (they now have a 2.4-MB line of code in their https://github.com/carpedm20/emoji/blob/645331c9474aaad7362ccb1d81279724514050fa/emoji/unicode_codes/data_dict.py).

#### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue

`bug.py`
```python
import timeit
print(timeit.timeit("import colorcet", number=1))
```

I have been looking for a way to enable image comparison by default, yet without success.

- I have read https://pytest-mpl.readthedocs.io/en/stable/configuration.html#enable-testing, which has nothing.
- I have tried adding `pytest_plugins = ("pytest_mpl.plugin",)` to `conftest.py`, but I am getting `ValueError: Plugin already registered under a different name: pytest_mpl.plugin=<module 'pytest_mpl.plugin' from 'C:\\ws\\project\\.venv\\Lib\\site-packages\\pytest_mpl\\plugin.py'>`
- I have looked into how `pytest` parses command-line arguments, but I haven't found a way to inject anything there.
- I have tried adding `import sys; sys.argv = [*sys.argv, "--mpl"]` to `conftest.py`.

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-11-06T09:48:32Z","updated_at":"2023-11-06T09:48:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

It seems that `maximum_sctype` is now deprecated, compare https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0052-python-api-cleanup.html#cleaning-up-the-main-namespace:

> All *sctype functions can be deprecated and removed, they (see [gh-17325](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/17325), [gh-12334](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/12334), and other issues for maximum_sctype and related functions).

Unfortunately,
- the migration guidelines on `maximum_sctype` is empty, https://numpy.org/devdocs/numpy_2_0_migration_guide.html#main-namespace
- https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/17325 and https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/12334 are not helpful, either
- I find no [other issues](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues?q=is%3Aissue+%22maximum_sctype%22) on `maximum_sctype` that are helpful
- `maximum_sctype` was removed in https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/24154 (note: "undocumented functions") although `maximum_sctype` [*is* documented](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.maximum_sctype.html)

In summary, when documented functions are deprecated, I'd expect some assistance in migration.

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-11-09T13:16:15Z","updated_at":"2023-11-09T13:16:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

(Cross-post from  https://stackoverflow.com/q/77451924/)

I am getting overflow `RuntimeWarning`s for an array of 9 elements but not for an array of 8 (or 200, ...) elements. It seems I get warnings iff I have more than 8 zeros and their number is not divisible by 4.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

# This works fine
np.zeros(1) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(4) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(7) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(8) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(12) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(16) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(100) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(200) / np.float64(1e-309)

# This gives "RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in divide"
np.zeros(9) / np.float64(1e-309)
```


### Error message:

```shell
<stdin>:1: RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in divide
```

(Cross-post from  https://stackoverflow.com/q/77451924/)

I am getting overflow `RuntimeWarning`s for an array of 9 elements but not for an array of 8 (or 200, ...) elements. It seems I get warnings iff I have more than 8 zeros and their number is not divisible by 4.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

# This works fine
np.zeros(1) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(4) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(7) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(8) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(12) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(16) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(100) / np.float64(1e-309)
np.zeros(200) / np.float64(1e-309)

# This gives "RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in divide"
np.zeros(9) / np.float64(1e-309)
```


### Error message:

```shell
<stdin>:1: RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in divide
```

Typing thinks `np.array("")` cannot be compared to `""`

### Reproduce the code example:

```python

import numpy as np
import numpy.typing as npt

X: npt.NDArray[np.str_] = np.array("")
reveal_type(X)
print(X > "")
print(X > X)
```

https://doc.qt.io/qtforpython-6/tutorials/basictutorial/signals_and_slots.html#the-slot-class says 

> We recommend marking all methods used by signal connections with a `@QtCore.Slot()` decorator. Not doing causes run-time overhead due to the method being added to the `QMetaObject` when creating the connection. This is particularly important for `QObject` classes registered with QML, where missing decorators can introduce bugs.

`NavigationToolbar2QT` does not do that.

### Code for reproduction

```python
from matplotlib.backends.backend_qt import NavigationToolbar2QT
from matplotlib.backends.backend_qtagg import FigureCanvasQTAgg
from PySide6.QtWidgets import QApplication

app = QApplication()
canvas = FigureCanvasQTAgg()
NavigationToolbar2QT(canvas)
```

mypy, pyright and PyLance all report the same error on the below code:

```
error: Operator "+" not supported for types "str | float" and "str | float"
    Operator "+" not supported for types "str" and "float" when expected type is "object"
    Operator "+" not supported for types "float" and "str" when expected type is "object" (reportGeneralTypeIssues)
1 error, 0 warnings, 0 informations
```

**To Reproduce**

```python
from __future__ import annotations

import numpy as np
import numpy.typing as npt
import pandas as pd

def get_series() -> pd.Series[str] | pd.Series[float]:
    return pd.Series(["0", "1"])

series = get_series()
print(series[0] + series[1])

def get_array() -> npt.NDArray[np.str_] | npt.NDArray[np.float64]:
    return np.array(["0", "1"])

array = get_array()
print(array[0] + array[1])
```

Since it works fine for `np.array` and is reported by two independent type checkers, I guess the stubs can use improving.

**Please complete the following information:**
 - python version: 3.11.5
 - version of type checker: mypy 1.5.1, pyright 1.1.328
 - version of installed `pandas-stubs`: 2.0.3.230814

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24506","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24506/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24506/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24506/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/24506","id":1862722029,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP85vBuHt","number":24506,"title":"ENH: char arrays auto-extending on indexed assignments","user":{"login":"bersbersbers","id":12128514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMTI4NTE0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12128514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers","html_url":"https://github.com/bersbersbers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-08-23T07:19:25Z","updated_at":"2023-08-23T07:19:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

I just spent an hour tracking down a bug related to appending a char array to another char array by using indexed assignments. Basically, most of my examples exhibited somewhat crazy behavior that took me a while to realize that they are all caused by the maximum length of the initial array. 

While `np.char.add` works fine, creating a new array with a new dtype (read, maximum length), indexed assignment don't return a new array. Also, concatenation is not the problem at all, it's the assignments themselves. This is consistent with how numeric arrays work in numpy, but in combination with the fact that numpy creates char arrays with the smallest type possible, this is surprising to say the least.

Take this example:

```python
import numpy as np

arr = np.array(["A", "A", "A", "AA"])
arr[0] = "BB"
print(arr[0])

arr = np.array(["A", "A", "A"])
arr[0] = "BB"
print(arr[0])
```

I would have expected `arr[0]` to be `'BB'` both times, but it's true only in the first case.

As I said, this is not uncommon in numpy. This example fails only in the second case:

```python
import numpy as np

arr = np.array([1 << 30, 1 << 31])
arr[0] = 1 << 31
print(arr[0])

arr = np.array([1 << 30, 1 << 30])
arr[0] = 1 << 31
print(arr[0])
```

Now, with numeric arrays, one hits this problem much less frequently, since numeric types are less granular and numpy does *not* choose the smallest dtype possible. E.g., `np.array(1).dtype` is `int32` and not `(u)int8`. By contrast, `np.array("1").dtype` is `<U1`.

Also, as you see above, some indexed assignments on numeric arrays overflow with an error; with char arrays, results are shortened *silently*.

For the record, here's a list of independent (I though!) real-life surprising behaviors caused by the current behavior:

```python
import numpy as np

X = "X"
LIST = [X] * 3
STR = X * 3

print("0) replacing fails as expected")
arr = np.array(["A", "B", "C"])
try:
    arr[arr != "C"] = LIST
except ValueError:
    print("Caught ValueError")
else:
    raise Exception("Expected ValueError")

print("1) replacing works despite length mismatch (should it fail?)")
arr = np.array(["A", "B", "C"])
arr[arr != "C"] = STR
print(arr)

print("2) appending does nothing (should print ['AX', 'BX', 'C'])")
arr = np.array(["A", "B", "C"])
arr[arr != "C"] = np.char.add(arr[arr != "C"], X)
print(arr)

print("3) appending works incompletely (should print ['AX', 'BBX', 'CCX'])")
arr = np.array([["A", "BB", "CC"]])
arr[arr != "C"] = np.char.add(arr[arr != "C"], f" {X}")
print(arr)

print("4) appending works incompletely (should print ['A X', 'BBB X', 'CCC X'])")
arr = np.array([["A", "BBB", "CCC"]], dtype="<U4")
arr[arr != "C"] = np.char.add(arr[arr != "C"], f" {X}")
print(arr)
```

I have a number of ideas to make this work:
- Do not autoselect the narrowest type possible upon creation of an array; by contrast, autoselect a wider type. (I must admit when proposing this, I had not expected that to have an impact on memory at all, but it seems that `x = np.array("1", dtype="<U500000000")` *does* allocate a low of memory.) Still, there is a reason for `np.array(1).dtype` to be `int32`, and that also has an impact on memory allocation.
- Allow auto-extension ("promotion"?) of the array on indexed assignments.
- Introduce a new auto-extending type. Again, before typing this, I had expected that `np.array("1", dtype=str)` would be that, but it seems simply that `np.array("1", dtype=str) == np.array("1", "<U1")`.

As a final alternative, I'd vote for throwing an error on indexed assignments that exceed the maximum length of the array.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24506/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24506/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-07-29T17:54:11Z","updated_at":"2023-07-29T17:54:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

Similar to https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/23796, and I hope it's not a false report again.

mypy 1.4.1 fails to type-check the code example below. Since `__isub__` works for all (signed or unsigned) integer types that I found, I think it should accept `np.integer` as well, not just `np.unsignedinteger`.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
# https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/23796

from typing import Any

import numpy as np
import numpy.typing as npt

# Mypy fail
result_i: npt.NDArray[np.integer[Any]] = np.arange(5, dtype=np.uint8)
result_i -= 5

# Mypy passes
result_u: npt.NDArray[np.unsignedinteger[Any]] = np.arange(5, dtype=np.uint8)
result_u -= 5

result_int = np.arange(5, dtype=int)
result_int -= 5

result_int_ = np.arange(5, dtype=np.int_)
result_int_ -= 5

result_intc = np.arange(5, dtype=np.intc)
result_intc -= 5

result_up = np.arange(5, dtype=np.uintp)
result_up -= 5

result_u8 = np.arange(5, dtype=np.uint8)
result_u8 -= 5

result_u16 = np.arange(5, dtype=np.uint16)
result_u16 -= 5

result_u32 = np.arange(5, dtype=np.uint32)
result_u32 -= 5

result_u64 = np.arange(5, dtype=np.uint64)
result_u64 -= 5

result_ip = np.arange(5, dtype=np.intp)
result_ip -= 5

result_i8 = np.arange(5, dtype=np.int8)
result_i8 -= 5

result_i16 = np.arange(5, dtype=np.int16)
result_i16 -= 5

result_i32 = np.arange(5, dtype=np.int32)
result_i32 -= 5

result_i64 = np.arange(5, dtype=np.int64)
result_i64 -= 5
```


### Error message:

```shell
bug.py:10: error: Invalid self argument "ndarray[Any, dtype[integer[Any]]]" to attribute function "__isub__" with type "Callable[[ndarray[Any, dtype[unsignedinteger[_NBit1]]], _SupportsArray[dtype[bool_ | unsignedinteger[Any]]] | _NestedSequence[_SupportsArray[dtype[bool_ | unsignedinteger[Any]]]] | bool | _NestedSequence[bool] | bool_ | int | integer[Any]], ndarray[Any, dtype[unsignedinteger[_NBit1]]]]"  [misc]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```

Summary

`pip install matplotlib` fails on Python 3.12.0b3, see https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/3935. This used to be blocked on https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/23808, but that is now fixed: `pip install https://anaconda.org/scientific-python-nightly-wheels/numpy/2.0.0.dev0/download/numpy-2.0.0.dev0-cp312-cp312-win_amd64.whl` works on Windows to install numpy 2.0.0.dev0. However, even then installation of matplotlib still fails.

Is there a way to install matplotlib on Python 3.12 betas?

### Proposed fix

I find this rather late TBH. From PEP 693:

> 3.12.0 candidate 1: Monday, 2023-07-31
3.12.0 candidate 2: Monday, 2023-09-04
3.12.0 final: Monday, 2023-10-02

This would mean waiting another five weeks to publicly release wheels, which is then the first point in time where many downstream developers will start even considering testing for Python 3.12 (I do not expect many to go through the pain of tracking down wheels if they are not available on PyPI). And from that point on, it's only roughly two months until the final release of 3.12.

I have always wondered how it could take months until even some popular libraries start supporting the latest Python version, and see this as a major hurdle to, as a user, installing and using the latest Python version by default. And I find that numpy, which is *very* close to the root of many Python environments' depedency tree, could be better example in this regard.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1608900934/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

See also https://stackoverflow.com/q/74552683/880783","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/22661/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/22661/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1326168983","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/22661#issuecomment-1326168983","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/22661","id":1326168983,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP85PC7uX","user":{"login":"bersbersbers","id":12128514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMTI4NTE0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12128514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers","html_url":"https://github.com/bersbersbers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-24T09:20:53Z","updated_at":"2022-11-24T09:20:53Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"I have been made aware that
```shell
python -c "import numpy as np; np.subtract(np.inf, np.inf)"  
```
raises a warning ("RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in subtract") while
```shell
python -c "import numpy as np; np.inf - np.inf"
```
does not.

So this issue has nothing to do with `reduce`, specifically.

---

In addition, I have come across a rather obscure example where two identically-looking arrays behave differently:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12128514/203734495-ccbb3727-3ab0-49ea-b8dc-d457368f4443.png)

The difference is visible only in the underlying bytes, and it allowed me to create these small examples:

```python
import numpy as np

y = np.frombuffer(b'\x00~', dtype=np.float16)
print(y)
y - 0  # emits no warning

x = np.frombuffer(b'\x00}', dtype=np.float16)
print(x)
x - 0  # emits warning
```

Similarly,
```python
import numpy as np

y = np.frombuffer(b'\x00\x00\xc0\x7f', dtype=np.float32)
print(y)
y - 0  # emits no warning

x = np.frombuffer(b'\x00\x00\xa0\x7f', dtype=np.float32)
print(x)
x - 0  # emits warning
```
and
```python
import numpy as np

y = np.frombuffer(b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\xf8\x7f', dtype=np.float64)
print(y)
y - 0  # emits no warning

x = np.frombuffer(b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\xf4\x7f', dtype=np.float64)
print(x)
x - 0  # emits warning
```

So in all examples, while both values are `NaN`, only the latter emits the warning. Checking the `float32` values, `0x7fc00000` and `0x7fa00000`, in https://www.h-schmidt.net/FloatConverter/IEEE754.html, both seem to be proper `NaN` values. [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NaN#Floating_point) confirms that 32-bit values of the form `s111 1111 1xxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx` (with `s` ignored) are NaN values if not all `x` are `0`. However, it mentions the difference between quiet and signaling `NaN`, and the difference between `x` and `y` above seems to be exactly that difference (`0x7fc00000` having the first, "quiet" bit set to 1, `0x7fa00000` has it set to `0`).

---

So this might be one explanation. Applying this analysis to the original examples, we see that both variants generate the same type of `NaN`, but only the latter emits a warning:

```python
import numpy as np

a = np.array(np.inf - np.inf)
a.tobytes()

b = np.subtract(np.inf, np.inf)
b.tobytes()
```

As `float32`, we see it is the quiet `NaN`:
```
import numpy as np

a = np.array(np.inf - np.inf, dtype=np.float32)
a.tobytes()

b = np.subtract(np.inf, np.inf, dtype=np.float32)
b.tobytes()
```

So that does not explain why a warning is emitted in this example.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1326168983/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

viewer = Viewer()

Problem

I use `PySide6` with `snake_case`:
https://doc.qt.io/qtforpython/feature-why.html#the-snake-case-feature

When debugging code in VS Code, I often get this:
> AttributeError: type object 'PySide6.QtWidgets.QFileDialog' has no attribute 'getSaveFileName'


### Proposed solution

In https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/0371cf971ff5c81a963683e3e1c2d48002929e21/lib/matplotlib/backends/qt_compat.py#L100, check if you need to use `QtWidgets.QFileDialog.get_save_file_name`","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/23816/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/23816/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/comments/1240390709","html_url":"https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/23816#issuecomment-1240390709","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/23816","id":1240390709,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABUios5J7tw1","user":{"login":"bersbersbers","id":12128514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMTI4NTE0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12128514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers","html_url":"https://github.com/bersbersbers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-08T08:23:17Z","updated_at":"2022-09-08T08:23:17Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> If you order the imports as:
> 
> ```python
> from __feature__ import snake_case  # isort: skip
> ...
> ```
> 
> does it not crash? 

It doesn't even run then:

> Exception has occurred: ModuleNotFoundError       (note: full exception trace is shown but execution is paused at: <module>)
> No module named '__feature__'

I came across some discussion related to this at https://bugreports.qt.io/browse/PYSIDE-1754?focusedCommentId=638930&page=com.atlassian.jira.plugin.system.issuetabpanels%3Acomment-tabpanel#comment-638930
For now, it seems one has to import `__feature__` after `PySide6`.

Fixing that, there is some freedom as to when to import `matplotlib`. I experimented with that a bit: This code

```python
import sys

from PySide6.QtWidgets import QApplication, QMainWindow  # isort: skip
from __feature__ import snake_case  # isort: skip

import matplotlib  # isort: skip
import matplotlib.backends.qt_compat  # isort: skip


class Viewer(QMainWindow):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()

        breakpoint()

        print("Hello, world!")


application = QApplication(sys.argv)
viewer = Viewer()
```

Problem

I use `PySide6` with `snake_case`:
https://doc.qt.io/qtforpython/feature-why.html#the-snake-case-feature

When debugging code in VS Code, I often get this:
> AttributeError: type object 'PySide6.QtWidgets.QFileDialog' has no attribute 'getSaveFileName'


### Proposed solution

In https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/0371cf971ff5c81a963683e3e1c2d48002929e21/lib/matplotlib/backends/qt_compat.py#L100, check if you need to use > Logarithm is a multivalued function: for each x there is an infinite number of z such that 10**z = x. The convention is to return the z whose imaginary part lies in [-pi, pi].
https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.log10.html

I wonder how `log10` can ever return both imaginary parts of pi and -pi. Since `np.imag(np.log(-1+0j)) == np.pi`, I'd say the correct interval should be (-pi, pi].

Pandas version checks

- [X] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
x = np.array([0.0, 1.0])
df = pd.DataFrame(x, columns=["x"])
np.log10(x, out=np.zeros_like(x), where=x>0)     # no warning, expected result ([0, 0])
np.log10(df.x, where=x>0)                        # no warning, usually incorrect result
np.log10(df.x, out=np.zeros_like(x), where=x>0)  # warning, correct result
```


### Issue Description

Warning:

> Python\Python310\lib\site-packages\pandas\core\arraylike.py:397: RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in log10
  result = getattr(ufunc, method)(*inputs, **kwargs)

### Expected Behavior

No warning

### Installed Versions

<details>

(`show_versions()` fails with assert '_distutils' in core.__file__, core.__file__)

Pandas 1.4.1 from pip and pandas-1.5.0.dev0+583.g5414a2d59 from GitHub

</details>

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/46473/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/46473/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/comments/1075955199","html_url":"https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/46473#issuecomment-1075955199","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/46473","id":1075955199,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA0YD85AIcX_","user":{"login":"bersbersbers","id":12128514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMTI4NTE0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12128514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers","html_url":"https://github.com/bersbersbers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bersbersbers/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-23T06:08:48Z","updated_at":"2022-03-23T06:08:48Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> The warning is being generated by NumPy, not pandas.

Correct - but it is somewhat specific to pandas in that the same does not happen for lists or tuples, while it does happen for pandas series and data frames:

```python
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
np.seterr(all="warn")

def fun(var):
    return np.log10(var, out=np.zeros_like(var), where=np.array(var) > 0)

x = [0.0, 1.0]

fun(x)                               # expected
fun(tuple(x))                        # expected
fun(np.array(x))                     # expected
fun(pd.Series(x))                    # correct result, unexpected warning
fun(pd.DataFrame(x, columns=["x"]))  # correct result, unexpected warning
```

-----

> Are you able to share a reproducible example along with the output you are stating is incorrect?

No. Due to the nature of uninitialized variables, it is impossible for me to ensure that the uninitialized memory is not zero for some reason or another, especially when it's reusing memory that was zeroed out before.

```python
import numpy as np
x = np.array([0.0, 1.0])
np.log10(x, where=x>0)
np.log10(x, where=x>0)
np.log10(x, where=x>0)
np.log10(x, where=x>0)
```

gives

```
>>> import numpy as np
>>> x = np.array([0.0, 1.0])
>>> np.log10(x, where=x>0)
array([2.99804668e+122, 0.00000000e+000])
>>> np.log10(x, where=x>0)
array([2.99804668e+122, 0.00000000e+000])
>>> np.log10(x, where=x>0)
array([2.99804668e+122, 0.00000000e+000])
>>> np.log10(x, where=x>0)
array([2.99804668e+122, 0.00000000e+000])
```

now, but it can be any other value in later runs. BUT, this behavior 

1. is not the issue I am reporting.
2. is completely independent of pandas.
3. is expected and documented in NumPy (https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.log10.html):
   > Note that if an uninitialized out array is created via the default out=None, locations within it where the condition is False will remain uninitialized.
4. was posted only to show that when I omit the `out` parameter, then the warning goes away, which made me think whether pandas/NumPy apply `np.log10` to the initialized `out` variable for some reason (which they should not).
   (This hypothesis is falsified by the warning also appearing in this example:
   ```python
   import numpy as np, pandas as pd
   x = np.array([0.0, 1.0])
   np.log10(pd.Series(x), where=x>0, out=np.ones_like(x))  # same warning
   ```

I hope that makes my original post clearer. Note that warnings are shown only once per session unless you do `np.seterr(all="warn")`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/comments/1075955199/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-03-23T06:31:10Z","updated_at":"2022-03-23T06:31:10Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

> Logarithm is a multivalued function: for each x there is an infinite number of z such that 10**z = x. The convention is to return the z whose imaginary part lies in [-pi, pi].
https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.log10.html

I wonder how `log10` can ever return both imaginary parts of pi and -pi. Since `np.imag(np.log(-1+0j)) == np.pi`, I'd say the correct interval should be (-pi, pi].

Pandas version checks

- [X] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
x = np.array([0.0, 1.0])
df = pd.DataFrame(x, columns=["x"])
np.log10(x, out=np.zeros_like(x), where=x>0)     # no warning, expected result ([0, 0])
np.log10(df.x, where=x>0)                        # no warning, usually incorrect result
np.log10(df.x, out=np.zeros_like(x), where=x>0)  # warning, correct result
```


### Issue Description

Warning:

> Python\Python310\lib\site-packages\pandas\core\arraylike.py:397: RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in log10
  result = getattr(ufunc, method)(*inputs, **kwargs)

### Expected Behavior

No warning

### Installed Versions

<details>

(`show_versions()` fails with assert '_distutils' in core.__file__, core.__file__)

Pandas 1.4.1 from pip and pandas-1.5.0.dev0+583.g5414a2d59 from GitHub

</details>
